title: There's no vaccine for hantavirus — what that means for outbreaks after the cruise ship
html_body:
Microorganisms in health, the environment and food systems |
View this email in your browser Thursday 7 May 2026

Hello Nature readers,
This week, we learn about outbreaks of hantavirus on a cruise ship and a parasitic fungus among a football team, and discover how mitochondria help fight off infections.
Medical staff arrive to evacuate passengers from MV Hondius, the cruise ship stricken by an outbreak of hantavirus. (AFP via Getty Images)

Hantavirus outbreak on cruise ship

An outbreak of hantavirus onboard the cruise ship MV Hondius has thrown a spotlight on the rare but deadly infection and the lack of options for treatment and prevention. For more than three decades, virologist Jay Hooper has been working to develop a vaccine against several strains of hantavirus that can infect people, including the Andes virus. He talks to Nature about why these vaccines remain elusive.

Nature | 7 min read

Mitochondria go fission for pathogens

Microbial infections kick off a process called mitochondrial fission, in which the organelle splits and multiplies — and researchers have now shown that this strengthens the host’s defenses. The team infected mouse macrophages and Caenorhabditis elegans nematodes with Escherichia coli bacteria and found that fission activated two antibacterial pathways: the mitochondrial unfolded protein response and inducible lipid droplet production. But bacteria such as Salmonella typhimurium stop mitochondrial fission when they infiltrate macrophages. And inhibiting a particular enzyme involved in fission in cells infected with S. typhimurium improved the cells’ ability to fight the infection.

The Scientist | 2 min read
 Reference: Science Immunology paper

Parasitic fungus plagues footy teams

More than 100 football players in Australia have been infected with Vittaforma corneaea, a rare, single-cell parasitic fungus that causes severe eye infections. The outbreak has been affecting multiple teams in the Northern Territory since March, but the parasite was identified as the cause just this week. The parasite mostly spreads through contaminated water or soil, and has caused outbreaks at swimming pools in Taiwan, in the Sea of Galilee, and among rugby players in Japan.

ABC News | 3 min read

Features and opinion
Researchers used genes from a microbe that naturally degrades 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT), a side product of the explosive TNT, to create an organism that can help clean up former munitions factories and war zones. The lighter-coloured wells indicate more DNT degradation. (Centro Nacional de Biotecnologia)

Tweaking microbes to fight pollution

Scientists are engineering bacteria to break down pollutants ranging from microplastics to soils laced with heavy metals. Synthetic biologists study naturally occurring microbes that are able to degrade or use contaminants, as well as the biochemistry that enables them to do it. Then, they use that knowledge to create organisms that do the job even better — inserting genes from one type of bacteria into another or creating genes essential for a specific application. “We have just begun to tap into the extraordinary capabilities of these tiny creatures,” says environmental engineer Ludmilla Aristilde.

Nature | 10 min read

An insider’s guide to citizen science

Two microbiome citizen science projects resulted in increased awareness of vaginal health and of sustainable soybean farming, and taught the scientists involved some valuable lessons on how to best use the approach in microbiome research. Microbiologists Sarah Lebeer and Sofie Goormachtig share what they learnt about engaging the public in research, the hands-on involvement these projects require, and the challenges of securing funding. But they say citizen science should not be mandatory, and works best when both science and society benefit.

Nature Microbiology | 10 min read

Spiky plastic tears viruses apart

A lightweight, flexible material covered in thousands of ultrafine spikes is smooth to the touch, but deadly for unsuspecting viruses. The researchers behind this thin acrylic film tested it against human parainfluenza virus, and found that 94% of virus particles that came into contact with it were torn apart or fatally damaged. “This new material offers a cheap, scalable way to make surfaces such as phones and hospital equipment far less likely to spread disease,” writes physicist and study co-author Elena Ivanova.

The Conversation | 3 min read
 Reference: Advanced Science paper

Quote of the week

“They were so excited to dive in… Their enthusiasm was wonderful to see.”

Microbiologist Anne Madden trialled her agar art kits for schoolchildren with sixth graders at a middle school in the United States. (The Scientist | 3 min read)

Today I learnt about not one, but two mud volleyball tournaments in Japan. The first is run in Tawaramoto Town, near Kyoto, and is part of the town’s autumn festival in June. Then in August, another tournament is happening in flooded rice paddies in Gujo, and promises players ‘skin-rejuvenating’ benefits from playing, thanks to bacteria in the soil.

Please send your thoughts or skincare tips to microbiology-briefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Rachel Fieldhouse, guest editor, Nature Briefing

summary.overview: 이번 이슈는 해양에서 발생한 헌트바이러스 감염 사례를 중심으로 질병 대응의 한계와 과학적 연구 동향을 조명하며, 미토콘드리아 기능, 곰팡이 감염, 미생물 활용 기술, 시민 과학, 플라스틱 바이러스 제거 기술 등 다양한 주제를 다룹니다.
covered_item_titles: An insider’s guide to citizen science

article_briefings.title: Hantavirus outbreak on cruise ship
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 크루즈선 MV Hondius에서 발생한 헌트바이러스 감염 사례가 치료·예방 방법 부족을 드러내며, 30년 이상 연구를 이어온 과학자의 노력이 조명되고 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: Mitochondria go fission for pathogens
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 미토콘드리아 분열이 병원균에 대한 숙주 방어를 강화한다는 연구 결과가 발표되며, 마우스와 쥐등 파충류를 대상으로 실험한 내용이 소개되었습니다.

article_briefings.title: Parasitic fungus plagues footy teams
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 호주 북부에서 축구 팀에 감염된 희귀 곰팡이 Vittaforma corneaea가 시각적 합병증을 유발하며, 감염 사례가 지속적으로 확인되고 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: Tweaking microbes to fight pollution
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 미생물 변형 기술이 미세플라스틱과 중금속 오염 물질 분해에 활용되며, 자연 발생 미생물의 생화학적 메커니즘 연구가 진행되고 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: An insider’s guide to citizen science
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 시민 과학 프로젝트가 여성 생식기 건강과 지속 가능한 대두 재배 인식 개선에 기여했으며, 미생물 연구에의 활용 전략이 제시되었습니다.

article_briefings.title: Spiky plastic tears viruses apart
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 수만 개의 미세 돌기를 가진 가벼운 플라스틱 필름이 94%의 바이러스를 제거하는 실험 결과가 공개되며, 바이러스 중和 기술의 잠재력이 조명되고 있습니다.
